article_title: A Linux Hardware Maker is Convincing Colorado to Leave Open Source Alone
core_thesis: Colorado's Age Attestation Bill (SB26-051) is set to be amended to exclude open source software, thanks to advocacy by System76 founder Carl Richell. This shows that community pressure can shape legislation, but similar bills in other states and at the federal level still threaten open source.
detailed_summary: Colorado's SB26-051, an age verification bill, originally applied to all software, but after months of advocacy by Carl Richell (System76 founder) working with co-author Senator Matt Ball, a proposed amendment will exclude open source operating systems, apps, code repositories (e.g., GitHub, GitLab), and containers (e.g., Docker, Podman). Richell is scheduled to testify on April 23 and is seeking community stories of kids benefiting from open source access. The bill passed the Colorado Senate 28-7 in March and awaits a House vote. Richell has created a template for other states to push for similar exemptions, but a federal bill (OS-level age verification) with no open source exemption could override state protections.

entities.name: Carl Richell
entities.role: Founder of System76, lead advocate for open source exemption

entities.name: Colorado Age Attestation Bill (SB26-051)
entities.role: Proposed law requiring age verification, now amended to exempt open source

entities.name: Matt Ball
entities.role: Colorado Senator and co-author of the bill, worked with Richell on amendments

entities.name: Neal Gompa
entities.role: Fedora contributor, inquired about outreach to other states

entities.name: Fosstodon
entities.role: Mastodon instance where advocacy updates were shared

key_data_points: Colorado's SB26-051 originally required age verification for all software, but a proposed amendment now excludes open source operating systems, apps, code repositories (GitHub, GitLab), and containers (Docker, Podman). (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: if you use Docker/Podman or host code on GitHub, this exemption protects your ability to access these tools without age gates.)

Carl Richell (System76 founder) worked directly with Senator Matt Ball to secure the amendment, demonstrating that direct advocacy with legislators can influence tech policy. (🟢 Relevant to your ambition: shows how one person's initiative can create a template for broader change.)

The bill passed the Colorado Senate 28-7 in March and now awaits a House vote; Richell is testifying on April 23 and asking the community for stories of kids building things with open source. (🟢 Relevant to your student perspective: highlights the educational value of unrestricted open source access.)

A proposed federal bill would require OS-level age verification across the US with no open source exemption, potentially overriding state-level protections. (🟢 Relevant to your cybersecurity/Red Teaming: such laws could impact how you access and use security tools; staying informed helps you anticipate restrictions.)
conclusions: Community advocacy can effectively shape legislation: Richell's persistent engagement with a bill co-author led to a targeted exemption for open source, proving that organized, informed voices can protect digital freedoms.

The fight is not over: while Colorado's bill may be amended, similar bills in other states and a federal proposal threaten open source. The template Richell created can be reused by others, but requires active participation from the open source community.

For users of Linux, containers, and code repositories, this exemption is critical to maintaining unfettered access to development and learning tools. As a student and homelabber, you rely on these resources; supporting such advocacy ensures they remain open.
personal_relevance_summary: This article directly impacts your homelab and DevOps interests: the exemption covers Docker, Podman, GitHub, and GitLab—tools you likely use daily. As a cybersecurity student, you also benefit from unrestricted access to open source security tools. The advocacy template is something you could adapt for your own state, aligning with your ambition to take initiative. Additionally, the article underscores the importance of community action in tech policy, a lesson applicable to your broader professional development.
extra_advice: Consider writing a letter to your state representatives using Carl Richell's template (available via his Fosstodon) to advocate for similar open source exemptions in your state. Also, compile a personal story of how open source software helped you learn programming or cybersecurity—this could be used as testimony if needed.
